text: Ureteroenteric anastomosis stricture represents one of the principal complications after radical cystectomy and the main cause of renal dysfunction after urinary diversion. The gold standard treatment consists on open revision in most cases and can result with significant morbidity. And the robotic approach can be a minimally invasive treatment option for these patients.
We present the clinical case of a sixty-four-year-old patient with past history of muscle-invasive bladder cancer who underwent robotic radical cystectomy in June, twenty-twenty-two.

The surgical technique for urinary diversion consists on an intracorporeal Y-shaped neobladder. Four months later, the patient presented with fever and bilateral lower back pain. The CT scan showed a mild left-sided ectasia up to the urethral enteric anastomosis and moderate right-sided ectasia with two centimeters of distal stenosis. The renogram showed proper function of both kidneys.
It was decided to perform a bilateral ureteral reimplantation using a robotical approach.

The patient is positioned in the supine and Trendelenburg position. Conventional placement of robotic trocars is visible. The surgeon begins by freeing pelvic adhesions, and instillation through the urethral catheter is performed to identify the knee or bladder.
The surgeon dissects the left ureter at both its distal and proximal portions, identifying a short stenosis at the level of the left ureteroenteric anastomosis.
The surgeon performs direct ureteral reimplantation, utilizing an exteriorized ureteral catheter to facilitate the procedure.
The surgeon frees and dissects the right ureter, identifying a stenosis. The ureter is confirmed to reach the bladder with tension.
The surgeon creates a new bladder flap and performs a body-type ureteral reimplantation using robotic assistance.
The patient presented a postoperative period with no incidences. In conclusion, robotical approach results a feasible option in selected patients.
